variables.bp_relationship: Inverse: higher daily step volume associated with lower SBP, DBP, MAP and pulse pressure, and lower MACE in hypertensives. Mechanism — chronic adaptation lowering peripheral resistance, improved endothelial/NO vasodilation, better baroreflex, reduced sympathetic tone; dose-dependent up to ~8,000–10,000 steps.
references: del Pozo Cruz et al. 2024, Eur J Prev Cardiol — UK Biobank hypertensives (n≈32,192): each +1,000 steps/day vs ~2,300 baseline ~17% lower MACE risk.
Chen et al. 2024, JMIR — higher wearable daily steps associated with lower SBP/DBP/MAP/pulse pressure in older adults; greater step variability linked to higher BP.

variables.bp_relationship: Inverse, dose-responsive: greater active energy / MVPA lowers resting BP and incident hypertension via chronic vascular remodeling + repeated acute post-exercise hypotension. Caveat: wrist active-kcal carries large error (MAPE often >20%).
references: Stamatakis et al. (ProPASS) 2025, Circulation (n=14,761) — reallocating time toward exercise-like activity yields clinically meaningful BP reductions (≥2 mmHg SBP).
Liu et al. 2017, Hypertension — ~6% lower hypertension risk per 10 MET-h/week of leisure-time activity.

variables.bp_relationship: Positive (opposite to activity metrics): higher resting/basal metabolic rate associated with higher BP independent of body size — elevated resting rate-pressure product, greater sympathetic activity, lower insulin sensitivity.
references: Weyer et al. 2000, Hypertension — obese hypertensives show significantly higher RMR vs BMI-matched normotensives.
MR study 2023, Sci Rep — genetically higher BMR causally raises heart failure / aortic aneurysm risk (adverse CV direction).

variables.bp_relationship: Inverse and intensity-graded: higher intensity lowers BP more per unit time; reductions scale with MET-minutes. Stronger acute post-exercise hypotension + chronic drop in peripheral resistance/arterial stiffness; HIIT especially effective.
references: Network meta-analysis 2023, Sci Rep — HIIT ~690 MET-min/week most effective for SBP (−5.07 mmHg); ~830 MET-min/week best for DBP (−4.42 mmHg).
Pescatello et al. (ACSM) — regular aerobic exercise lowers BP ~5–7 mmHg in hypertensives.

variables.bp_relationship: Inverse: accumulating exercise duration (~150 min/week) lowers resting SBP/DBP. Acute — each 20–60 min bout >40% VO2peak triggers post-exercise hypotension up to ~24 h; chronic — sustained ambulatory BP reduction.
references: Resistant-HTN RCT 2021 (PMC8340008) — 12 wk of 3×40-min/week reduced 24-h and daytime ambulatory SBP/DBP.
Halliwill 2001, post-exercise hypotension review — PEH after submaximal dynamic exercise persists for hours (up to ~13 h).

variables.bp_relationship: Inverse, energy-driven: greater distance lowers incident hypertension and SBP/DBP; at matched energy expenditure walking and running give comparable benefit.
references: Williams & Thompson 2013, ATVB (n≈49,000) — running cut incident hypertension 4.2% and walking 7.2% per MET-h/day; comparable at equal energy.
Walking intervention 2018 (PMC6119598) — 6-month guided walking lowered SBP up to −21.3 mmHg in those with baseline SBP >160.

variables.bp_relationship: Inverse: habitual stair climbing lowers SBP and arterial stiffness, increases leg strength; more flights/day associated with lower CVD risk. Brief vigorous weight-bearing load reduces arterial stiffness (key SBP mediator).
references: Hong et al. 2018 (Korean postmenopausal stage-2 HTN) — 12-wk stair-climbing reduced arterial stiffness and BP; stiffness drop partly explained SBP improvement.
Tulane Mediterranean cohort — climbing >5 flights/day associated with ~20% lower ASCVD risk.
